output:
# DRAG to TAPER: Identity-Anchored, Token-Budget-Annealed Visual Connectors that Exact-Collapse to the Full-Patch Projection for Sample-Efficient Multimodal Instruction Tuning

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Large multimodal models (LMMs) are now built from three reusable parts: a pretrained visual backbone that turns an image into a grid of patch features [7,8], a pretrained large language model that consumes a sequence of embeddings, and a vision-language cross-modal CONNECTOR that maps the visual features into the LLM word-embedding space so that image content can be attended to as if it were text [1,2]. The backbone and the LLM are typically inherited and often kept frozen or lightly tuned; the connector is the component that carries the burden of cross-modal alignment, and it is also where designs diverge most sharply. Because the connector both decides how much visual information enters the LLM and how that information is aligned to language, its inductive biases dominate training cost and convergence behavior.

Two connector families dominate. The first is the FULL-PATCH PROJECTOR popularized by LLaVA and refined in LLaVA-1.5 [1,2]: a simple MLP applied independently to every patch feature, emitting N visual tokens (one per patch) into the LLM. It is information-preserving (no patch is dropped) and easy to optimize, so it aligns quickly with modest data; its cost is the long visual-token sequence of length N, which lengthens every training iteration and every inference forward pass. The second family is the QUERY-BASED RESAMPLER: BLIP-2's Q-Former [3] and Flamingo's Perceiver Resampler [4,5] introduce a small set of learned query tokens that cross-attend to the patch features and emit a compressed set of M < N visual tokens. Resamplers cut the LLM-side sequence length and therefore the per-iteration compute, which is attractive for scaling. Early instruction-following LMMs such as MiniGPT-4 [16] and mPLUG-Owl [17] instantiate one or the other of these two connector families.

Both families are trained under the same TWO-STAGE protocol established by LLaVA, whose pioneering pipeline leverages text-only GPT-4 [19] to synthesize visual instruction data, and adopted by LLaVA-1.5 [2]: stage one is vision-language alignment PRETRAINING on image-text pairs (ranging from roughly 600K curated pairs up to billion-scale web corpora), which teaches the connector to place visual content in the LLM embedding space; stage two is visual INSTRUCTION TUNING on conversational and task-formatted multimodal data, which teaches the model to follow visual instructions. A documented data-quality caveat is that naive merging of academic VQA datasets can cause the model to overfit to short-answer VQA and lose natural conversational ability, a failure also observed when academic VQA data is merged into instruction tuning [2,6], so the protocol and the data mix, not just the architecture, govern the final model.

Within this shared protocol there is a documented CONVERGENCE / SAMPLE-EFFICIENCY GAP. The LLaVA-1.5 work reports that, although resamplers reduce the visual-token count, they "cannot achieve convergence as efficiently as LLaVA with a comparable amount of training data," attributing this to the resampler's larger count of trainable parameters [2]. In other words, at equal data the compressing connector reaches a worse alignment than the information-preserving one, and the authors explicitly flag "the development of a sample-efficient visual resampler" as an open direction for scaling instruction-following multimodal models.

The critical observation is that this rich body of work treats the resampler as a FIXED-FROM-INITIALIZATION module and MEASURES the resulting tax (slower convergence, more data to reach parity) without structurally CURING it. Reported benchmark tables give final downstream accuracy at a fixed training budget and a fixed token budget, which marginalizes over the training trajectory and the budget axis and therefore hides the sample-efficiency deficit rather than removing it. No deployed connector gives the compressing resampler an identity setting that reproduces the full-patch projection's fast-aligning head start; the head start is left on the table precisely where it would help most.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the underlying failure DRAG (Detached-Resampler Alignment-convergence Gap). DRAG is the gap created when a compressing connector is born as a random, lossy bottleneck detached from the full-patch alignment manifold that the simple projector reaches easily. Because the resampler's query tokens and cross-attention start from scratch, the model must simultaneously discover (a) a content-selection policy over patches and (b) the cross-modal alignment into the LLM space, two coupled problems, with no anchor to the already-working full-patch projection. The full-patch projector solves only (b) and forwards all content, so it converges fast; the resampler pays a structural tax to solve (a) and (b) jointly from a cold start. This is the mechanism behind the LLaVA-1.5 Introduction's reported symptom, and it is a property of initialization and parameterization, not of any individual dataset.

DRAG is SILENT under standard evaluation. VQA/GQA/benchmark-suite accuracy is read off at the end of a fixed training budget and at a single fixed token budget, so the metric integrates over exactly the trajectory where the deficit lives. A resampler that needs more steps or more data to reach the same alignment appears merely "slightly lower" or "comparable," and the missing column, sample efficiency / convergence rate at matched data, is never plotted. The tax is therefore real but unattributed: the field has the measurement (final accuracy) that obscures the very quantity (convergence) it elsewhere admits is the problem. The harm vanishes in only one degenerate regime: when the connector is initialized on the full-patch alignment manifold so that, at init, it equals the full-patch projection of all N patches.

TAPER (Token-budget Annealed Projection with Exact Reproduction) is built to occupy exactly that regime and then move off it safely. It has four coupled pieces. (1) An IDENTITY PATH equal to the LLaVA-1.5 full-patch MLP projection over all N patches, warm-started from the deployed projector weights, so the model begins on the working alignment manifold. (2) A RESIDUAL COMPRESSOR R, gated by alpha, that learns what to merge or drop as an edit on top of the identity path rather than as a from-scratch bottleneck, in the spirit of residual learning [29]. (3) An ANNEALED TOKEN-BUDGET schedule B(t): N -> M that introduces compression gradually, so the model never has to align and compress cold at the same time; alignment is inherited, compression is annealed in as a curriculum [30]. (4) An EXACT-COLLAPSE guarantee: at alpha = 0, B = N the output is token-for-token identical to the full-patch projection, gated by E_collapse <= 1e-6. Convergence at the default is therefore identical to LLaVA-1.5 by construction, and any compression learned thereafter is strictly additive machinery that starts from a working point.

The exact-collapse identity is explicit. Let X = [x_1, ..., x_N] be the N vision-encoder patch features for an image, and let P be the LLaVA-1.5 full-patch MLP projector. The deployed baseline produces visual tokens T_LLaVA = [P(x_1), ..., P(x_N)]. TAPER produces T_TAPER = P(X) + alpha * R(X, B), where R is the residual compression operator and B is the token budget, arranged so that at the identity default (alpha = 0, B = N) we have T_TAPER[i] = P(x_i) for all i = 1..N: the residual term is gated off and the budget admits all N tokens, so the output values, the loss, and the gradients are bit-for-bit those of LLaVA-1.5's full-patch projection. Define E_collapse = max over a held-out probe set of the per-token normalized L2 difference between T_TAPER(alpha=0, B=N) and T_LLaVA; the build fails unless E_collapse <= 1e-6. This turns "strictly safe generalization" from an empirical hope into a machine-checkable, regression-proof property.

The novelty is the JOIN of a four-clause wedge, not any single clause. C1 (CONNECTOR-ONLY, SAME PROTOCOL AND DATA): TAPER acts purely as the vision-language connector between the standard vision encoder and the LLM, reusing LLaVA-1.5's exact two-stage protocol and the exact same data, with no vision-backbone change, no LLM-architecture change, and no new pretraining corpus. C2 (ANNEALED TOKEN-BUDGET CURRICULUM): compression is conditioned on an annealed schedule B(t): N -> M, a controllable curriculum spanned by one model, not a single fixed ratio frozen from step 0. C3 (RESIDUAL ON AN INFORMATION-PRESERVING IDENTITY PATH): compression is learned as a residual on a warm-started identity path, so the model edits a working alignment instead of rediscovering one. C4 (PROVABLE EXACT-COLLAPSE TO THE FULL-PATCH BASELINE): at the identity default the connector provably collapses, token-for-token, to the LLaVA-1.5 full-patch projection, guaranteeing zero regression against the strong baseline. Each prior family, full-patch projectors, query resamplers, and token pruning/merging, satisfies at most two of these clauses; the conjunction is precisely what converts the field's measured-but-uncured DRAG tax into a structurally cured, regression-proof connector.

Several boundary cases pin down the design. (A) At alpha = 0, B = N, TAPER is exactly LLaVA-1.5 (the identity anchor and CI reference). (B) At alpha = 1, B = M, TAPER emits M < N tokens, achieving the resampler's efficiency goal, but its alignment is an edit of the inherited full-patch alignment rather than a cold-start bottleneck (the intended deployment operating point). (C) A degenerate "mean-pool init" to M tokens does NOT reproduce the full-patch projection: it is already lossy at step 0, sits off the alignment manifold, and reintroduces the joint align-and-compress cold start, so E_collapse is large and the CI gate rejects it; this is the concrete counterexample showing that "small token count at init" is insufficient and that only an information-preserving identity at init removes DRAG. (D) If the schedule is pinned at B = N forever, the model can still learn a same-length residual refinement but never reduces tokens, provably never regressing below LLaVA-1.5; this is the safe-generalization floor. (E) At intermediate schedule points the single trained model spans a continuum of token budgets, exposing the convergence-versus-compression trade-off as a controllable, directly measurable axis, surfacing the very column standard benchmarks marginalize away.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can replacing the from-scratch lossy query resampler with an identity-anchored, token-budget-annealed connector that provably exact-collapses to the LLaVA-1.5 full-patch MLP projection (alpha = 0, B = N, E_collapse <= 1e-6) recover full-patch sample-efficiency and convergence while operating at a compressed visual-token budget M < N?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1 (Resamplers are THE scaling lever). The visual connector decides how many tokens the language model must attend to per image, and that token count is the single most leveraged knob in an instruction-following large multimodal model (LMM). A full-patch projector like LLaVA-1.5 [2] forwards N patch embeddings (576 for a CLIP ViT-L/14-336 [7,8] grid), so the visual sequence dominates the prompt: it inflates self-attention cost quadratically, caps the number of images that fit in context, and throttles training throughput. Query-based resamplers (Q-Former [3], Perceiver Resampler [4,5]) exist precisely to collapse N patches to M << N tokens, which is what makes cheaper training, faster inference, longer effective context, and multi-image / video reasoning feasible. But the LLaVA-1.5 authors flagged the catch in their own Introduction: resamplers "cannot achieve convergence as efficiently as LLaVA with a comparable amount of training data," likely because their extra trainable parameters must be learned from scratch. That convergence penalty is the DRAG gap. Curing DRAG is interesting because it unblocks the exact scaling path the source paper called for: a sample-efficient resampler that buys token compression WITHOUT paying a convergence tax.

P2 (Turns a measured-but-uncured phenomenon into a structural fix, and promotes a marginalized axis). The field has repeatedly MEASURED that compressing connectors converge slower or need larger stage-1 pretraining, but treats this as folklore to be amortized with more compute, not as a defect to be cured. TAPER reframes it: DRAG is structural (a random, lossy bottleneck DETACHED from the pretrained full-patch -> LLM alignment manifold), so the fix is structural (initialize the connector ON that manifold and learn compression as a residual). The deeper methodological contribution is what this exposes: sample efficiency and convergence become a FIRST-CLASS, plottable axis (accuracy vs training-tokens, accuracy vs visual-token-budget), instead of being marginalized away by final-accuracy benchmark tables that report a single converged number at a fixed budget. Standard tables (VQAv2 [20], GQA [21], TextVQA [22], MME [25], MMBench [26], POPE [27], SEED-Bench [28]) are SILENT about training-budget cost because they integrate over it. Making that axis visible is a contribution beyond any single architecture.

P3 (Exact-collapse / warm-start-from-baseline is a general recipe). The principle underneath TAPER generalizes far past one projector: if you want to adopt ANY compressing module on top of a working uncompressed system, initialize it so that at its identity default it reproduces the strong baseline token-for-token (E_collapse <= 1e-6, CI-gated), then anneal compression up as a residual on the information-preserving identity path. This is a "ship-in-shadow with provable zero regression, then dial up compression" discipline. It is reusable for audio and video tokenizers, multi-image fusion, and long-context vision connectors, and it is exactly the kind of safe-adoption recipe practitioners lack today: most compressors are introduced as from-scratch replacements that must re-earn alignment, so adopting them is a gamble. Inheriting the baseline's convergence and accuracy as a provable floor turns that gamble into a monotone improvement path.

P4 (Practical impact and democratization). Concretely, fewer visual tokens means cheaper training (less compute to converge, because the identity warm start removes the cold-start tax), lower inference latency and higher throughput (attention over M instead of N tokens), and smaller memory footprint (more images or longer dialogues per context window). Because TAPER is connector-only (C1) and warm-started from the deployed projector, a team that already runs LLaVA-1.5 can drop it in without re-pretraining the vision backbone or LLM and without a new corpus. That lowers the compute barrier to reach parity, which democratizes capable LMMs for groups without frontier-scale budgets, and it directly serves edge and mobile multimodal deployment, where token budget is the binding constraint and where lightweight projector work (MobileVLM-style LDP [31], Qwen-VL's resampler [18]) is already a live concern.

P5 (Safety and quality angle). Compression is not free for fidelity: dropping visual tokens trades away fine-grained detail, which is precisely where LMMs hallucinate and where OCR / document / chart grounding breaks (limitation 4 in LLaVA-1.5; measurable on TextVQA [22], DocVQA [23], and hallucination via POPE [27]). In high-stakes domains such as medical or scientific imaging, an opaque, fixed-ratio compressor is a liability because you cannot bound its regression. TAPER's annealed token budget B(t): N -> M (C2) plus its provable exact-collapse to the full-patch projection (C4) give practitioners a budget-controllable, regression-floored connector: they can trade tokens for fidelity along a known curve, and they always have a provable SAFE DEFAULT (alpha = 0, B = N) that is bit-comparable to the strong full-patch baseline. A connector whose worst case is "no worse than LLaVA-1.5" is a meaningfully safer object to deploy than one whose worst case is unknown.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 (BINDING, REPRESENTATIONAL). The naive fix "just initialize the resampler by mean-pooling patches or with random learned queries" cannot reproduce the full-patch projection at step 0: a mean-pool over N=576 patches collapses spatial detail before any gradient flows, and random queries emit an arbitrary M-token summary that has zero relationship to the pretrained full-patch -> LLM alignment manifold. Formally, a from-scratch M < N query bottleneck has NO parameter setting whose output equals "forward all N patches through the learned projector P(X)" token-for-token; the function classes are not nested, so there is no identity element to warm-start from. This means the model is already lossy and off-manifold at init, and it must re-learn content selection AND cross-modal alignment jointly from a cold start, exactly the DRAG pathology. Removing DRAG is therefore not a hyperparameter-tuning problem; it structurally REQUIRES an information-preserving identity path that exactly equals P(X) plus a residual compressor layered on top. This forces clauses C3 (residual on an information-preserving identity path, warm-started) and C4 (provable exact-collapse anchor).

O2 (ANNEALING SCHEDULE). The budget anneal B: N -> M does not act in isolation: every token retained costs LLM sequence length, attention compute, and a slice of the gradient signal, so the schedule trades convergence speed against the very efficiency the method promises. Anneal too fast and the model discards tokens before the residual compressor has learned what to keep, destroying the warm-start advantage; anneal too slow and you pay full-patch compute for most of training, negating the sample-efficiency claim. The gate alpha and the budget B are coupled, raising alpha while B is still large double-counts information, while raising B-pressure while alpha is near zero starves the compressor of learning signal, so they must be co-scheduled, not tuned independently. A single fixed compression ratio applied from step 0 cannot express this. This forces clause C2 as a principled cosine co-schedule curriculum rather than a fixed N -> M ratio.

O3 (DISENTANGLING COMPRESSION FROM ALIGNMENT). When a compressed model underperforms full-patch LLaVA, the deficit could come from two distinct causes, genuine information loss (the M tokens cannot carry the needed evidence) or misalignment (the tokens carry the evidence but sit off the LLM's expected manifold), and ordinary accuracy tables cannot separate them. Without an in-model control that is provably identical to the full-patch path, every comparison confounds these two failure modes, and attribution becomes guesswork. The exact-collapse setting (alpha=0, B=N) provides exactly that control: it is the same architecture, same weights, same forward graph, differing only in the residual being switched off, so any gap that appears as alpha and budget move is attributable to compression rather than implementation drift. This reframes exact-collapse from a mere safety net into a MEASUREMENT INSTRUMENT. This forces clause C4 to exist as an in-model ablation anchor, not just a correctness guarantee.

O4 (CAPABILITY HETEROGENEITY ACROSS BUDGET). Visual tasks do not share a single information requirement: OCR-heavy and dense-document tasks (TextVQA, DocVQA) need many tokens to preserve fine glyph-level evidence, while coarse scene-level VQA (VQAv2, GQA) tolerates aggressive compression with little loss. A single fixed compression ratio is therefore guaranteed to be wrong for some task family, either over-compressing dense tasks or wasting tokens on coarse ones. The method must be budget-conditional, letting one trained model serve multiple operating points, and it must be evaluated per budget rather than at one ratio. This forces clause C2 (one model spanning budgets) together with budget-stratified evaluation at M in {576, 256, 144, 64, 36}.

O5 (HALLUCINATION / GROUNDING INTERACTION). Compression is not capability-neutral: dropping tokens can remove the specific grounding evidence the model needs to verify an object's presence, which tends to increase object hallucination measured by POPE. A method that improves average accuracy while silently raising hallucination would be a regression disguised as progress, and aggregate benchmark scores would hide it. Hallucination must therefore be measured explicitly at each token budget and treated as a first-class outcome rather than assumed flat. This forces token-budget-stratified POPE reporting as part of validation and the Responsible-AI note.

O6 (METRIC BLINDNESS). Average final-accuracy tables marginalize over both the training budget (how much data was seen) and the token budget (how many tokens the connector emitted), so DRAG, a tax that lives entirely in sample efficiency and convergence, is invisible in exactly the tables the field reports. Two connectors that converge at wildly different rates can show identical final accuracy at 100% data, making the gap unmeasurable by construction. Exposing DRAG and verifying its fix requires accuracy-vs-training-data convergence curves and token-budget-stratified accuracy as PRIMARY, first-class metrics, not appendix afterthoughts. This forces the Q5 metric design (AUCC and convergence-tax as primary, budget-stratified accuracy throughout); without these the contribution cannot be confirmed or refuted.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The wedge requires C1 AND C2 AND C3 AND C4 JOINTLY: a connector-only drop-in (C1) that anneals a token-budget curriculum N -> M (C2), learns compression as a RESIDUAL on an information-preserving identity path warm-started from the full-patch projector (C3), and provably EXACT-COLLAPSES to the LLaVA full-patch MLP projection at its identity default with zero regression (C4). Every prior line covers at most TWO of these clauses. The combination is unoccupied not by accident but because the field treated compression and convergence as separate problems: compression work optimized the token-vs-accuracy frontier and ignored the cold-start convergence tax, while the one sample-efficient connector (the full-patch projector) did no compression at all. No one tied compression to a warm start on the uncompressed alignment manifold, so no one inherited convergence while compressing.

Near-miss 1 - Q-Former / BLIP-2 (Li et al., ICML 2023) [3]. A learned querying transformer with a fixed set of query tokens that resamples frozen image features into M tokens for the LLM. Covers C1 (it is a connector). Misses C2 (the query count is fixed, not an annealed curriculum, one ratio set from step 0), C3 (the queries are a RANDOM, LOSSY bottleneck trained from scratch, this IS the DRAG source), and C4 (no collapse to the full-patch projection; it needs a large stage-1 representation-learning pretrain to even align). Misses C2, C3, C4.

Near-miss 2 - Perceiver Resampler / Flamingo (Alayrac et al., NeurIPS 2022) [4]; Perceiver / Perceiver-IO (Jaegle et al., 2021/2022) [5]. A fixed bank of latent queries cross-attends to a variable number of visual features and emits a fixed-length latent set. Covers C1 (connector). Misses C2 (fixed latent count, no budget curriculum), C3 (latents are learned from scratch, a detached lossy bottleneck, not a residual on an identity path), C4 (no exact-collapse to a full-patch projector). Misses C2, C3, C4.

Near-miss 3 - LLaVA / LLaVA-1.5 full-patch MLP projector (Liu et al., 2023) [1,2]. A 1- or 2-layer MLP that maps every patch embedding into the LLM token space; it is sample-efficient and is the COLLAPSE TARGET TAPER inherits. Covers C1 (connector) and is the very baseline whose convergence we want to keep. Misses C2 (no token budget at all, always N tokens) and C3 (no compression, so no residual-on-identity compressor); C4 is vacuous for it (there is nothing to collapse to). Misses C2, C3.

Near-miss 4 - Token pruning / merging: ToMe (Bolya et al., ICLR 2023) [10]; PruMerge / LLaVA-PruMerge (Shang et al., 2024) [11]. Reduce visual tokens by merging or pruning similar/low-salience tokens, often training-free and applied post-hoc to an already-trained model. Covers C2-adjacent (they do move along the budget axis). Misses C3 (heuristic similarity merging, not a learned residual on a warm-started identity path) and C4 (no provable exact-collapse and no convergence inheritance; they are bolted onto a trained model and do NOT cure the from-scratch convergence tax, they manage it after the fact). Misses C3, C4.

Near-miss 5 - Honeybee C-/D-Abstractor (Cha et al., CVPR 2024) [12]; TokenPacker (Li et al., 2024) [15]. Locality-preserving / flexible abstractors (convolutional or deformable) that compress patches to a chosen number of tokens while keeping spatial locality. Covers C1 (connector) and C2-adjacent flexibility (the output count is configurable). Misses C3 (no identity warm-start residual, the abstractor is trained from scratch, detached from the full-patch manifold) and C4 (no exact-collapse to the full-patch MLP projection, no provable zero-regression default). Misses C3, C4.

Near-miss 6 - Matryoshka Multimodal / nested visual tokens (Cai et al., 2024) [13]; Matryoshka Representation Learning (Kusupati et al., NeurIPS 2022) [14]. Train nested/granular visual token representations so one model can be queried at multiple token budgets at inference. Covers C1 (connector) and C2 (genuinely budget-spanning, one model, many budgets, the closest prior on the curriculum axis). Misses C3 (the nested tokens are trained from scratch; there is no identity residual warm-started from the deployed full-patch projector) and C4 (no exact-collapse to the full-patch projection and no convergence inheritance; the smallest budget is not bit-comparable to LLaVA-1.5, and the largest does not provably equal the full-patch projector). Misses C3, C4.

Near-miss 7 - MobileVLM LDP / lightweight projectors [31]; Qwen-VL resampler (Bai et al., 2023) [18]. Efficient connector variants (depthwise/lightweight downsample projectors, or a learned resampler) aimed at smaller or faster deployment. Covers C1 (connector). Misses C2 (no annealed budget curriculum), C3 (no identity-path residual warm-started from a full-patch projector), and C4 (no provable exact-collapse / zero-regression safe default). Misses C2, C3, C4.

Crisp summary. TAPER is the first to combine C1+C2+C3+C4: connector-level, token-budget-annealed (N -> M curriculum), compression-as-a-residual-on-a-warm-started-identity-path, and provable exact-collapse to the LLaVA full-patch MLP projection. Three explicit coverage gaps remain open across the literature: (i) NO warm-start / exact-collapse safe-generalization recipe for compressing connectors, every compressor is introduced as a from-scratch replacement that must re-earn alignment rather than inherit it; (ii) sample-efficiency / convergence is NOT reported as a first-class metric, final-accuracy tables marginalize over training budget, so the DRAG tax stays invisible; (iii) NO residual-on-identity-path compressor exists, the budget-spanning lines (Matryoshka, Honeybee, abstractors) all train from scratch and none provably reduce to the strong full-patch baseline at any budget. TAPER occupies exactly that empty intersection.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Q5a - METHOD. TAPER connector internals: T_TAPER(X) = P(X) + alpha * R(X, B), a drop-in replacement for the LLaVA-1.5 connector. Identity path P = the deployed LLaVA-1.5 two-layer MLP projector applied to all N=576 patch embeddings X from CLIP ViT-L/14-336 [7,8,2], warm-started directly from released LLaVA-1.5 projector weights so that, with the residual disabled, the forward graph is bit-for-bit the published model. Residual compressor R = a Perceiver/Q-Former-style cross-attention block with M learned queries that attend over the N patch keys/values and emit M compressed tokens [3,4], initialized so its contribution is near-zero at the start (small output projection), keeping the identity path dominant early. Gate alpha = scalar (or per-token) gate in [0,1] scaling the residual; alpha=0 yields pure identity. Token budget B and the N<->M mechanism: a budget-selection operator S_B is applied around the additive form; S_B emits B tokens by keeping the top-(B-M) highest-importance identity tokens (learned importance/merge score) and appending the M residual-compressed tokens that ToMe-style soft-merge the remaining identity tokens [10]; both the appended residual block and the merge are gated jointly by alpha and by the budget, so that at B=N the selection is the identity (no token is merged or replaced and all N identity tokens pass through unchanged), at alpha=0 the residual contributes exactly zero, and at B=M,alpha=1 only the M compressed tokens remain. The boundary B=N,alpha=0 therefore yields T_TAPER = P(X) token-for-token (all N identity tokens), and intermediate B interpolates by progressively merging more identity tokens into the residual summary. Co-schedule: a cosine schedule co-anneals alpha:0->1 and B:N->M over a configurable fraction (e.g. first 40-70%) of stage-2 instruction-tuning steps. Base LLM = Vicuna-7B and Vicuna-13B [9], trained under LLaVA-1.5's exact two-stage recipe with identical data and hyperparameters. EXACT-COLLAPSE: at alpha=0, B=N, output == full-patch projection P(X) token-for-token; E_collapse = max over a held-out probe set of the per-token normalized L2 difference between T_TAPER and P(X), required <= 1e-6, enforced by a CI gate that fails the build if violated (clause C4).

Q5b - DATASETS. Training: LLaVA-1.5 stage-1 pretrain = 558K BLIP-captioned subset of CC/SBU/LAION; stage-2 = the 665K instruction-tuning mixture [2]. Evaluation: VQAv2 [20], GQA [21], TextVQA [22], DocVQA [23], ScienceQA-IMG [24], MME [25], MMBench and MMBench-CN [26], POPE [27], SEED-Bench [28]. Convergence sweeps: stage-2 trained on training-data fractions {10, 25, 50, 100}% and logged along training steps to build accuracy-vs-data and accuracy-vs-step curves.

Q5c - METRICS (PRIMARY explicit). PRIMARY = SAMPLE EFFICIENCY = accuracy-vs-training-data-seen convergence curve at fixed budget M, summarized by Area-Under-Convergence-Curve (AUCC); plus the "convergence tax" = the extra training data a from-scratch resampler needs to reach 95% of TAPER's accuracy at the same M. Secondary: token-budget-stratified accuracy at M in {576, 256, 144, 64, 36} across all eval benchmarks; training iteration time and inference latency/throughput per M; POPE hallucination per budget [27]; E_collapse (<= 1e-6).

Q5d - BASELINES. B1 LLaVA-1.5 MLP full-patch (N=576), the exact-collapse target, converges fast, expensive inference [2]. B2 Q-Former-from-scratch resampler with M queries [3]. B3 Perceiver Resampler [4]. B4 ToMe / PruMerge token reduction applied to a trained LLaVA [10,11]. B5 Honeybee C-Abstractor [12]. B6 Matryoshka Multimodal [13]. B7 TAPER (ours).

Q5e - ABLATIONS. Identity path on vs off; warm-start vs random init of the identity path; annealing none vs fast vs slow; residual (P + alpha*R) vs replacement (R only); budget M sweep {576, 256, 144, 64, 36}; freeze-identity-then-unfreeze vs fully joint training.

Q5f - VALIDATION. A hard exact-collapse CI gate enforces E_collapse <= 1e-6 on every build (clause C4). Three falsifiable predictions: P1 - at the identity default (alpha=0, B=N), TAPER reproduces LLaVA-1.5 logits and decisions within E_collapse; any divergence above 1e-6 falsifies the construction. P2 - driven to a target budget M, TAPER reaches a given accuracy with materially LESS training data than B2/B3, and the gap is largest EARLY in training (the sample-efficiency win); if from-scratch resamplers match TAPER's early-training curve, P2 is falsified. P3 - at matched token budget M, TAPER >= B2/B3/B4 on dense/OCR tasks (DocVQA, TextVQA) and is not worse on POPE; if TAPER is worse on dense/OCR or raises hallucination at equal M, P3 is falsified.

Q5g - EXPECTED OUTCOMES (HYPOTHESES, numeric ranges, NOT proven results). E_collapse <= 1e-6 by construction (the only near-certain item, since it is enforced). At M=144, roughly 2x-4x fewer image-text pairs than from-scratch Q-Former to reach 95% of full-patch VQAv2 accuracy (hypothesized). Roughly 2x-4x training/inference speedup vs N=576 with an average benchmark drop of about 1-3 points (hypothesized). Smaller drop on TextVQA/DocVQA than from-scratch resamplers at equal M (hypothesized). POPE not worse than full-patch (hypothesized). Convergence-curve dominance concentrated EARLY in training (hypothesized). Advantage tapering toward ~0 as B -> N, since TAPER collapses to full-patch there (expected by design). All items are framed as hypotheses to be confirmed or refuted by the protocol above.

Q5h - RESPONSIBLE-AI / DEPLOYMENT. The compression-vs-hallucination tradeoff is validated PER BUDGET and disaggregated, with special attention to dense/OCR and safety-critical settings (e.g. medical document reading) where dropped tokens can remove decisive evidence; token-budget-stratified POPE is reported rather than aggregate hallucination. The exact-collapse CI gate doubles as a safety / ship-in-shadow mechanism: a deployment can run TAPER in identity mode (provably == LLaVA-1.5) and enable compression only after per-budget validation. Dataset and benchmark licensing (LAION/CC/SBU/COCO sources and individual benchmark licenses for VQAv2/GQA/TextVQA/DocVQA/ScienceQA/MME/MMBench/POPE/SEED) must be respected. For critical applications, keep a human in the loop as a check rather than the sole arbiter, and never report only aggregate accuracy when a lower token budget may concentrate failures on specific task families. Dataset provenance and content filtering: the LAION/CC/SBU pretraining data is web-scraped and known to contain PII, NSFW imagery, and demographic representation biases, so license compliance must be verified per source, known NSFW/harmful pairs filtered out before training, and any release must acknowledge that residual corpus biases may propagate into the connector's learned attention. Fairness disaggregation: evaluation should extend beyond task families to report OCR/TextVQA accuracy stratified by script (Latin vs non-Latin) and VQA accuracy stratified by geographic/cultural context, noting that token-budget compression may disproportionately degrade non-English text, low-resource visual contexts, and images under-represented in Western-centric corpora. Safety-critical bound: TAPER must not serve as a standalone clinical or safety-critical decision system; medical or regulatory deployment requires prospective validation and approval, with the identity default (alpha=0, B=N) mandated as the starting operating point until per-budget validation on the target clinical distribution is complete. Dual-use awareness: by lowering per-image inference cost, TAPER reduces the economic barrier to large-scale visual understanding, so the intended use is efficient multimodal research and edge deployment, and authors and adopters should prohibit application to mass surveillance, unauthorized biometric processing, or bulk visual profiling without informed consent via an acceptable-use clause in any public release. Environmental honesty: while TAPER reduces per-image compute at a given operating point, the net effect depends on whether efficiency induces greater total deployment volume (the Jevons effect), so system cards should report absolute compute (GPU-hours, estimated CO2e) alongside per-token efficiency metrics and avoid claiming unconditional sustainability benefit without accounting for deployment scale.
